Here's some always rules. Labeling negative emotions or dynamics always diffuses those negative emotions or dynamics. Labeling positive emotions or dynamics always reinforces those positive emotions or dynamics. Always. This is going to scare you regarding the negatives because you're used to the reaction you get from denying them. Let's use an example. Say you're dealing with someone and you know they see you as being unpredictable or as some may say a loose cannon. The change will be from your gut instinct of wanting to deny it and saying, I don't want you to think I'm a loose cannon. To the label of it seems like I'm a loose cannon. A two millimeter shift. Now if you wanted to label a positive present in this dynamic, you would say, it seems like you value stability or it seems like you value predictability. These are the flip side positives and negatives of identifying the same dynamic. Now we've just addressed the fact that labels always reinforce positives and always reduces negatives. However, that doesn't address the degree of impact. It might affect them a little. It might affect them a lot. But it might not be obvious at first. Just because it looks like it didn't have an effect or you got silence, that doesn't mean it didn't work. You just need more of that label. While the concept of labeling is pretty easy to understand, how you use this tactic will be a major part of how successful you are. If this tactic is deployed effectively, you will be able to identify and then slowly shift the inner voice of your counterpart to be both more collaborative and trusting of you. In order to fully grasp the concept of how to effectively use labeling, it's also important to have some more background information in human psychology. People's emotions have two levels. First is the presenting behavior, which is the visible part above the surface that you can see and hear, basically tone of voice, facial expressions, body language, etc. The second is the underlying feeling that motivates the behavior, which is beneath the surface. This part is much more complex because it might not even show up on the surface or it might manifest itself in different ways. For example, have you ever had someone you were close to who you knew was upset with you? But when you asked them if they were upset, they said, I'm fine. How did you handle that situation?
